/**
 * POLARIS v6 API client.
 *
 * Mirrors the FastAPI contract in src/polaris_v6/api/{runs,health,stream}.py.
 * NEXT_PUBLIC_BACKEND_URL points to the FastAPI dev server (default
 * http://127.0.0.1:8000 for local development).
 */

const BACKEND_URL =
  process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_BACKEND_URL ?? "http://127.0.0.1:8000";

export type TemplateId =
  | "clinical"
  | "trade"
  | "housing"
  | "defense"
  | "climate"
  | "ai_sovereignty"
  | "canada_us"
  | "workforce";

export type RunStatus =
  | "queued"
  | "in_progress"
  | "completed"
  | "cancelled"
  | "failed"
  | "abort_scope_rejected"
  | "abort_corpus_inadequate"
  | "abort_corpus_approval_denied"
  | "abort_no_verified_sections";

export interface RunRequest {
  template: TemplateId;
  question: string;
  document_ids?: string[];
}

export interface RunStatusResponse {
  run_id: string;
  status: RunStatus;
  template: string;
  question: string;
  queued_at: string;
  started_at?: string | null;
  finished_at?: string | null;
}

export interface ApiError extends Error {
  status: number;
  body: unknown;
}

async function asJsonOrThrow<T>(response: Response): Promise<T> {
  const body = await response.json().catch(() => null);
  if (!response.ok) {
    const error = new Error(
      `POLARIS backend returned ${response.status}`,
    ) as ApiError;
    error.status = response.status;
    error.body = body;
    throw error;
  }
  return body as T;
}

export async function createRun(
  payload: RunRequest,
): Promise<RunStatusResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/runs`, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "content-type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify(payload),
  });
  return asJsonOrThrow<RunStatusResponse>(response);
}

export async function getRun(runId: string): Promise<RunStatusResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/runs/${runId}`);
  return asJsonOrThrow<RunStatusResponse>(response);
}

export type DataClassification =
  | "PUBLIC_SYNTHETIC"
  | "CAN_REAL"
  | "PRIVATE"
  | "CLIENT"
  | "UNKNOWN";

export interface UploadResponse {
  document_id: string;
  filename: string;
  bytes: number;
  sha256: string;
  classification: DataClassification;
  parse_status: "queued" | "completed" | "failed";
  chunk_preview: string[];
  content?: string;
  html?: string;
}

export async function getUpload(document_id: string): Promise<UploadResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(
    `${BACKEND_URL}/upload/${encodeURIComponent(document_id)}`,
  );
  return asJsonOrThrow<UploadResponse>(response);
}

export async function uploadDocument(
  file: File,
  classification: DataClassification = "UNKNOWN",
): Promise<UploadResponse> {
  const form = new FormData();
  form.append("file", file);
  form.append("classification", classification);
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/upload`, {
    method: "POST",
    body: form,
  });
  return asJsonOrThrow<UploadResponse>(response);
}

export type ScopeVerdict = "accepted" | "needs_clarification" | "rejected";
export type RefusalReason =
  | "clinical_treatment_recommendation"
  | "individual_legal_advice"
  | "individual_financial_advice"
  | "personal_political_endorsement"
  | "out_of_template_scope";

export interface ScopeDecision {
  verdict: ScopeVerdict;
  template: string;
  question: string;
  rationale: string;
  refusals: RefusalReason[];
  intended_source_tiers: ("T1" | "T2" | "T3")[];
}

export interface AmbiguityCluster {
  cluster_id: number;
  representative_text: string;
  member_source_ids: string[];
}

export interface AmbiguityResult {
  is_ambiguous: boolean;
  clusters: AmbiguityCluster[];
  fallback_used: boolean;
}

export interface AmbiguityCandidate {
  source_id: string;
  text: string;
}

export async function checkAmbiguity(
  question: string,
  candidates: AmbiguityCandidate[],
): Promise<AmbiguityResult> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/ambiguity`, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "content-type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      question,
      candidates,
      min_cluster_size: 2,
      similarity_threshold: 0.5,
    }),
  });
  return asJsonOrThrow<AmbiguityResult>(response);
}

export async function checkScope(
  template: TemplateId,
  question: string,
): Promise<ScopeDecision> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/scope/check`, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "content-type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify({ template, question }),
  });
  return asJsonOrThrow<ScopeDecision>(response);
}

  sample_questions: string[];
  out_of_scope_examples: string[];
}

export async function listTemplates(): Promise<TemplateContent[]> {

  return asJsonOrThrow<TemplateContent[]>(response);
}

  run_id: string;

  queued_at: string;
  finished_at: string;

    frame_id: string;
    frame_name: string;

    contradiction_id: string;
    section_id: string;

    claim_b: string;
    evidence_a: string[];
    evidence_b: string[];
    resolution: string;
  }[];

  generator_model: string;
  verifier_model: string;
  family_segregation_passed: boolean;
}

export async function getBundle(runId: string): Promise<EvidenceContract> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/runs/${runId}/bundle`);
  return asJsonOrThrow<EvidenceContract>(response);
}

export type ChartType = "forest_plot" | "comparison_table" | "timeline";

export interface VegaLiteSpec {
  $schema: string;
  title?: string;
  data: { values: Record<string, unknown>[] };
  mark?: unknown;
  encoding?: unknown;
  layer?: unknown;
  polaris_provenance: {
    chart_type: ChartType;
    evidence_ids: string[];
    [k: string]: unknown;
  };
  [k: string]: unknown;
}

export async function getChart(
  runId: string,
  chartType: ChartType,
): Promise<VegaLiteSpec> {
  const response = await fetch(
    `${BACKEND_URL}/runs/${runId}/charts/${chartType}`,
  );
  return asJsonOrThrow<VegaLiteSpec>(response);
}

export function downloadBundleAsJson(bundle: EvidenceContract): void {
  const blob = new Blob([JSON.stringify(bundle, null, 2)], {
    type: "application/json",
  });
  const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
  const link = document.createElement("a");
  link.href = url;
  link.download = `polaris_bundle_${bundle.run_id}.json`;
  document.body.appendChild(link);
  link.click();
  document.body.removeChild(link);
  URL.revokeObjectURL(url);
}

export interface StreamEvent {
  event: string;
  data: Record<string, unknown>;
}

/**
 * Subscribe to /stream/{runId}. Returns the EventSource so the caller can
 * close it. Per Phase 0 stub, the backend yields 5 deterministic events.
 */
export function subscribeToRun(
  runId: string,
  onEvent: (event: StreamEvent) => void,
  onError?: (error: Event) => void,
): EventSource {
  const source = new EventSource(`${BACKEND_URL}/stream/${runId}`);

  const handler = (event: MessageEvent<string>) => {
    try {
      const parsed = JSON.parse(event.data);
      onEvent({ event: event.type, data: parsed });
    } catch {
      onEvent({ event: event.type, data: { raw: event.data } });
    }
  };

  for (const eventName of [
    "scope_decision",
    "retrieval_progress",
    "verifier_verdict",
    "section_complete",
    "run_complete",
  ]) {
    source.addEventListener(eventName, handler);
  }

  if (onError) {
    source.onerror = onError;
  }
  return source;
}

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Slice 001 — Clinical Scope Discovery + Ambiguity Detection
// Mirrors src/polaris_graph/scope/scope_decision.py + api/intake_route.py.
// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

export type IntakeStatus =
  | "in_scope"
  | "ambiguous_needs_clarification"
  | "out_of_scope"
  | "refused";

export type IntakeScopeClass =
  | "clinical_efficacy"
  | "clinical_safety"
  | "clinical_diagnosis"
  | "clinical_prognosis"
  | "out_of_scope";

export type PicoAxisName = "population" | "intervention" | "outcome";

export interface IntakeAmbiguityAxis {
  axis: PicoAxisName;
  plausible_interpretations: string[];
  needs_clarification: boolean;
}

export interface IntakeScopeDecision {
  status: IntakeStatus;
  scope_class: IntakeScopeClass | null;
  ambiguity_axes: IntakeAmbiguityAxis[];
  clarifications_needed: string[];
  provenance: Record<string, unknown>;
  decision_id: string;
  decided_at_utc: string;
  latency_ms: number;
  needs_disambiguation?: boolean;
  candidate_snippets?: { text: string; embedding: number[] }[];
}

export type DisambiguationCluster = {
  cluster_id: number;
  label: string;
  sample_snippets: string[];
};

export interface DisambiguationResponse {
  is_ambiguous: boolean;
  num_clusters: number;
  clusters: DisambiguationCluster[];
  server_time_utc: string;
}

export async function runDisambiguation(
  candidates: { text: string; embedding: number[] }[],
): Promise<DisambiguationResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/api/disambiguation`, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "content-type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify({ candidates }),
  });
  return asJsonOrThrow<DisambiguationResponse>(response);
}

export interface IntakeSuccessResponse {
  error: false;
  decision: IntakeScopeDecision;
  server_time_utc: string;
}

export interface IntakeErrorBody {
  error: true;
  code: "too_short" | "too_long" | "invalid_input";
  message: string;
  raw: string;
}

export class IntakeBadRequestError extends Error {
  code: IntakeErrorBody["code"];
  raw: string;
  constructor(body: IntakeErrorBody) {
    super(body.message);
    this.name = "IntakeBadRequestError";
    this.code = body.code;
    this.raw = body.raw;
  }
}

export async function runIntake(
  question: string,
): Promise<IntakeSuccessResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/api/intake`, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "content-type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify({ question }),
  });

  if (response.status === 400) {
    const detail = await response.json().catch(() => null);
    const body = (detail?.detail ?? detail) as IntakeErrorBody | null;
    if (body && body.error) {
      throw new IntakeBadRequestError(body);
    }
  }

  return asJsonOrThrow<IntakeSuccessResponse>(response);
}

export interface IntakeHealthResponse {
  status: "ok";
  slice: string;
  pipeline_stages: string[];
}

export async function getIntakeHealth(): Promise<IntakeHealthResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/api/intake/health`);
  return asJsonOrThrow<IntakeHealthResponse>(response);
}

export type RetrievalSourceTier = "T1" | "T2" | "T3";

export interface RetrievalSource {
  source_id: string;
  url: string;
  domain: string;
  tier: RetrievalSourceTier;
  title: string;
  publication_date: string | null;
  authors: string[];
  snippet: string;
  full_text_available: boolean;
  full_text: string | null;
  fetched_at_utc: string;
  provenance: Record<string, unknown>;
  retracted?: boolean;
}

export interface RetrievalAdequacyVerdict {
  is_adequate: boolean;
  sources_per_tier: Record<RetrievalSourceTier, number>;
  min_required_per_tier: Record<RetrievalSourceTier, number>;
  failure_reason: string | null;
}

export interface EvidencePool {
  pool_id: string;
  decision_id: string;
  sources: RetrievalSource[];
  adequacy: RetrievalAdequacyVerdict;
  queries_executed: string[];
  retrieval_started_at_utc: string;
  retrieval_finished_at_utc: string;
  latency_ms: number;
  cost_usd: number;
}

export interface RetrievalSuccessResponse {
  error: false;
  pool: EvidencePool;
  server_time_utc: string;
}

export interface RetrievalErrorBody {
  error: true;
  code: "wrong_status" | "wrong_scope_class" | "fetch_backend_unavailable";
  message: string;
  decision_id: string | null;
}

export class RetrievalBadRequestError extends Error {
  code: RetrievalErrorBody["code"];
  decision_id: string | null;
  constructor(body: RetrievalErrorBody) {
    super(body.message);
    this.name = "RetrievalBadRequestError";
    this.code = body.code;
    this.decision_id = body.decision_id;
  }
}

export async function runRetrieval(
  decision: IntakeScopeDecision,
): Promise<RetrievalSuccessResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/api/retrieval`, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "content-type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify({ decision }),
  });

  if (response.status === 400) {
    const detail = await response.json().catch(() => null);
    const body = (detail?.detail ?? detail) as RetrievalErrorBody | null;
    if (body && body.error) {
      throw new RetrievalBadRequestError(body);
    }
  }

  return asJsonOrThrow<RetrievalSuccessResponse>(response);
}

export interface RetrievalHealthResponse {
  status: "ok";
  slice: string;
  pipeline_stages: string[];
  fetch_backend: string;
}

export async function getRetrievalHealth(): Promise<RetrievalHealthResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/api/retrieval/health`);
  return asJsonOrThrow<RetrievalHealthResponse>(response);
}

/** Convenience: count sources matching a given tier. */
export function countSourcesByTier(
  pool: EvidencePool,
  tier: RetrievalSourceTier,
): number {
  return pool.sources.filter((s) => s.tier === tier).length;
}

/** Convenience: dedupe + sort sources by tier (T1 first), then by domain. */
export function sortSourcesByTier(
  sources: RetrievalSource[],
): RetrievalSource[] {
  const tier_rank: Record<RetrievalSourceTier, number> = {
    T1: 0,
    T2: 1,
    T3: 2,
  };
  return [...sources].sort((a, b) => {
    const t = tier_rank[a.tier] - tier_rank[b.tier];
    if (t !== 0) return t;
    return a.domain.localeCompare(b.domain);
  });
}

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Slice 003 — Generator with strict-verify
// Mirrors src/polaris_graph/generator2/verified_report.py +
// api/generation_route.py.
// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

export type SectionStatus = "verified" | "regenerated" | "dropped";

export type PipelineVerdict = "success" | "abort_no_verified_sections";

export type DropReason =
  | "invalid_token"
  | "span_out_of_range"
  | "numeric_mismatch"
  | "overlap_too_low"
  | "no_provenance_token";

export interface ReportVerifiedSentence {
  section_id: string;
  sentence_text: string;
  provenance_tokens: string[];
  verifier_pass: boolean;
  drop_reason: DropReason | null;
  evaluator_agrees: boolean | null;
  is_synthesis_claim?: boolean;
  assertion_surface?: AssertionSurface;
}

export type AssertionSurface =
  | "prose"
  | "table"
  | "summary_bullet"
  | "limitation"
  | "caption"
  | "heading";

export interface VerifiedReportSection {
  section_id: string;
  section_title: string;
  verified_sentences: ReportVerifiedSentence[];
  section_verify_pass_rate: number;
  section_status: SectionStatus;
}

export interface VerifiedReport {
  report_id: string;
  pool_id: string;
  decision_id: string;
  sections: VerifiedReportSection[];
  overall_verify_pass_rate: number;
  pipeline_verdict: PipelineVerdict;
  generator_model: string;
  evaluator_model: string;
  family_segregation_passed: boolean;
  verifier_pass_threshold: number;
  started_at_utc: string;
  finished_at_utc: string;
  latency_ms: number;
  cost_usd: number;
}

export interface GenerationSuccessResponse {
  error: false;
  report: VerifiedReport;
  server_time_utc: string;
}

export interface GenerationErrorBody {
  error: true;
  code:
    | "inadequate_pool"
    | "completion_backend_unavailable"
    | "malformed_output";
  message: string;
  pool_id: string | null;
  decision_id: string | null;
}

export class GenerationBadRequestError extends Error {
  code: GenerationErrorBody["code"];
  pool_id: string | null;
  decision_id: string | null;
  constructor(body: GenerationErrorBody) {
    super(body.message);
    this.name = "GenerationBadRequestError";
    this.code = body.code;
    this.pool_id = body.pool_id;
    this.decision_id = body.decision_id;
  }
}

export async function runGeneration(
  pool: EvidencePool,
  scope_class?: string | null,
): Promise<GenerationSuccessResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/api/generation`, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "content-type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify({ pool, scope_class: scope_class ?? null }),
  });

  if (response.status === 400) {
    const detail = await response.json().catch(() => null);
    const body = (detail?.detail ?? detail) as GenerationErrorBody | null;
    if (body && body.error) {
      throw new GenerationBadRequestError(body);
    }
  }

  return asJsonOrThrow<GenerationSuccessResponse>(response);
}

export interface GenerationHealthResponse {
  status: "ok";
  slice: string;
  pipeline_stages: string[];
  completion_backend: string;
}

export async function getGenerationHealth(): Promise<GenerationHealthResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/api/generation/health`);
  return asJsonOrThrow<GenerationHealthResponse>(response);
}

/** Filter to non-dropped sections (verified + regenerated). */
export function keptSections(report: VerifiedReport): VerifiedReportSection[] {
  return report.sections.filter((s) => s.section_status !== "dropped");
}

/** Filter sentences to those that passed strict_verify. */
export function keptSentences(
  section: VerifiedReportSection,
): ReportVerifiedSentence[] {
  return section.verified_sentences.filter((s) => s.verifier_pass);
}

// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Slice 004 — Audit bundle (GPG-signed .tar.gz export)
// Mirrors src/polaris_graph/api/audit_bundle_route.py.
// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

export interface AuditBundleErrorBody {
  error: true;
  code:
    | "fk_chain_mismatch"
    | "verdict_not_success"
    | "cited_span_unreachable_after_snapshot"
    | "copyrighted_span_in_bundle"
    | "gpg_unavailable"
    | "sign_failed";
  message: string;
  report_id: string | null;
}

export class AuditBundleError extends Error {
  code: AuditBundleErrorBody["code"];
  report_id: string | null;
  constructor(body: AuditBundleErrorBody) {
    super(body.message);
    this.name = "AuditBundleError";
    this.code = body.code;
    this.report_id = body.report_id;
  }
}

/**
 * Build + download a GPG-signed audit bundle for the given BPEI chain.
 *
 * Returns a Blob of the audit_<id>.tar.gz on success. On HTTP 4xx/5xx
 * with a structured error body, throws AuditBundleError with the .code
 * field set so callers can show specific UX (e.g. "GPG signer not
 * configured" vs "report verdict not success").
 */
export async function downloadAuditBundle(
  decision: IntakeScopeDecision,
  pool: EvidencePool,
  report: VerifiedReport,
): Promise<Blob> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/api/audit-bundle`, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "content-type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify({ decision, pool, report }),
  });
  if (!response.ok) {
    const detail = await response.json().catch(() => null);
    const body = (detail?.detail ?? detail) as AuditBundleErrorBody | null;
    if (body && body.error) {
      throw new AuditBundleError(body);
    }
    throw new Error(`Audit bundle request failed: HTTP ${response.status}`);
  }
  return await response.blob();
}

export interface BundlePreviewByContentType {
  count: number;
  bytes: number;
}

export interface BundlePreviewResponse {
  preview_bundle_id: string;
  generator_model: string;
  polaris_version: string;
  file_count: number;
  total_bytes: number;
  content_type_breakdown: Record<string, BundlePreviewByContentType>;
}

export async function previewAuditBundle(
  decision: IntakeScopeDecision,
  pool: EvidencePool,
  report: VerifiedReport,
): Promise<BundlePreviewResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/api/audit-bundle/preview`, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "content-type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify({ decision, pool, report }),
  });
  if (!response.ok) {
    const detail = await response.json().catch(() => null);
    const body = (detail?.detail ?? detail) as AuditBundleErrorBody | null;
    if (body && body.error) {
      throw new AuditBundleError(body);
    }
    throw new Error(`Bundle preview request failed: HTTP ${response.status}`);
  }
  return (await response.json()) as BundlePreviewResponse;
}

export interface AuditBundleHealthResponse {
  status: "ok";
  slice: string;
  pipeline_stages: string[];
  signing_backend: string;
}

export async function getAuditBundleHealth(): Promise<AuditBundleHealthResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/api/audit-bundle/health`);
  return asJsonOrThrow<AuditBundleHealthResponse>(response);
}

export type BenchmarkDimension =
  | "sourcing_tier_mix"
  | "numeric_grounding"
  | "provenance_density"
  | "refusal_correctness"
  | "coverage_completeness"
  | "latency"
  | "auditability";

export interface BenchmarkSystemScores {
  system: string;
  by_dimension: Record<BenchmarkDimension, number | null>;
  evidence: Record<BenchmarkDimension, string[]>;
}

export interface BenchmarkQuestionScores {
  question_id: string;
  question_text: string;
  is_refusal_bait: boolean;
  polaris: BenchmarkSystemScores;
  chatgpt: BenchmarkSystemScores;
  gemini: BenchmarkSystemScores;
}

export interface BenchmarkAggregateScoreboard {
  polaris_mean: Record<BenchmarkDimension, number | null>;
  chatgpt_mean: Record<BenchmarkDimension, number | null>;
  gemini_mean: Record<BenchmarkDimension, number | null>;
  n_questions: number;
}

export interface BenchmarkScoreboard {
  benchmark_id: string;
  ran_at_utc: string;
  per_question: BenchmarkQuestionScores[];
  aggregate: BenchmarkAggregateScoreboard;
  polaris_wins: number;
  external_wins: number;
  ties: number;
}

export interface BenchmarkHealthResponse {
  status: "ok";
  slice: string;
  results_root: string | null;
  available_benchmarks: string[];
}

export const BENCHMARK_DIMENSION_LABELS: Record<BenchmarkDimension, string> = {
  sourcing_tier_mix: "Sourcing tier mix",
  numeric_grounding: "Numeric grounding",
  provenance_density: "Provenance density",
  refusal_correctness: "Refusal correctness",
  coverage_completeness: "Coverage completeness",
  latency: "Latency",
  auditability: "Auditability",
};

export const ALL_BENCHMARK_DIMENSIONS: BenchmarkDimension[] = [
  "sourcing_tier_mix",
  "numeric_grounding",
  "provenance_density",
  "refusal_correctness",
  "coverage_completeness",
  "latency",
  "auditability",
];

export async function getBenchmarkHealth(): Promise<BenchmarkHealthResponse> {
  const response = await fetch(`${BACKEND_URL}/api/benchmark/health`);
  return asJsonOrThrow<BenchmarkHealthResponse>(response);
}

export async function getBenchmarkScoreboard(
  benchmark_id: string,
): Promise<BenchmarkScoreboard> {
  const response = await fetch(
    `${BACKEND_URL}/api/benchmark/${encodeURIComponent(benchmark_id)}/scoreboard`,
  );
  return asJsonOrThrow<BenchmarkScoreboard>(response);
}

export async function getBenchmarkSummary(
  benchmark_id: string,
): Promise<string> {
  const response = await fetch(
    `${BACKEND_URL}/api/benchmark/${encodeURIComponent(benchmark_id)}/summary`,
  );
  if (!response.ok) {
    throw new Error(`benchmark summary fetch failed: HTTP ${response.status}`);
  }
  return await response.text();
}

.codex\I-f4-004\codex_diff_audit.txt:2031:.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_13\codex_stderr.log:2802:outputs\polaris_graph\GEMINI_E2E_20260316_092353_report.md:1:# Comparative Analysis of Adhesion Testing Methods for Polymer Coatings: Methodologies, Performance, and Regulatory Compliance ## Abstract This research report presents a comparative analysis of adhesion testing methods for polymer coatings, specifically evaluating pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. Spanning 15 sections and comprising 16,701 words, the study examines standardized test method definitions within ASTM and ISO frameworks alongside material suitability limits and thickness constraints. The methodology involves a rigorous review of ASTM D4541 protocols for pull-off testing and ASTM D903 and ISO11339 compliance for peel test variations, including 90-degree and T-peel configurations. Furthermore, the analysis addresses knife and scrape test methods for hard coatings, surface contaminants, and adhesion performance scoring scales. Key findings highlight the impact of environmental temperature and immersion effects on adhesion, alongside theoretical adhesion modeling and cohesive failure analysis. The report classifies failure modes and mechanism units while assessing urethane adhesive capabilities and bond strength applications. Administrative testing documentation and warranty requirements are also scrutinized to ensure regulatory compliance. Drawing upon 84 total unique sources cited and incorporating 218 total citations in report, this work establishes critical guidelines for selecting appropriate testing methodologies. The implications suggest that strict adherence to standardized protocols enhances reliability in predicting coating performance under varying environmental conditions, thereby informing industry best practices for quality assurance and warranty validation in polymer coating applications. ## Standardized Test Method Definitions and ASTM ISO Frameworks Adhesion testing frameworks for polymer coatings are governed by a complex array of ASTM and ISO standards that define methodological rigor and compliance thresholds [1]. Additionally, these standardized protocols establish the baseline for comparing pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials [2]. Furthermore, the regulatory landscape distinguishes between quantitative tensile measurements and qualitative visual assessments, each serving specific diagnostic purposes within the coating lifecycle [3]. Pull-off adhesion testing applies tensile force perpendicular to the substrate to measure coating resistance to separation [1]. This method is utilized to gage adhesive strength of the coating once applied and cured as per manufacturer's guidelines [2]. Moreover, the pull-off adhesion tester measures adhesion of coatings to metal, wood, concrete, and rigid substrates, providing versatility across material classes [4]. Pull-off test data provides quantitative indication of bond tensile strength [5]. In addition, industrial-grade adhesives typically have tensile strength ranging from 10 MPa to over 60 MPa depending on formulation [6]. Hydroxyapatite coatings showed repeatable adhesion strength of 3.4 ± 1.0 MPa across both ASTM test methods [7]. ASTM C633-01 test uses the reported value diameter cylindrical blocks with 38 mm length for adhesion testing. Moreover, ASTM D4541 and BS EN ISO 4624 describe pull-off adhesion testing standards for protective coatings [8]. The pull-off test is standardized by ISO 4624:2023 and is widely used to quantify adhesion [9]. ASTM D7234 defines the standard test method for pull-off adhesion strength on concrete using portable testers [10]. Pull-off testing follows ISO 4624, ASTM D4541, and D7234 standards for evaluating coating peel strength [11]. Valid pull-off test requires coating to cover at least 50% of stamp surface after detachment [11]. In addition, the Cross-Cut Test provides qualitative evaluation while the Pull-Off Method provides quantitative evaluation of coating adhesion [3]. This distinction is critical when selecting methods for epoxy, urethane, or silicone systems, as quantitative data may be required for structural warranty validation. Additionally, cross-Cut Test classifies adhesion results into six levels according to the degree of peeling after tape removal [3]. The procedure involves making lattice-pattern cuts through the coating to the substrate, applying adhesive tape, and peeling in one motion [3]. ASTM D3359 outlines standardized test methods for evaluating adhesion of coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape [12]. This standard is widely recognized for evaluating coating adhesion on metal substrates using the tape test method [13]. ISO 2409 specifies the cross-cut test method for evaluating adhesion of coatings on sheet metal parts [13]. ASTM D3359 defines adhesion as molecular attraction and mechanical bonds between coating and substrate [14]. ASTM D3359 procedures assess the adhesion of coating films specifically on metallic substrates [15]. The tape test involves applying and removing pressure-sensitive tape over cuts made in the film [15]. The tape test is qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesion [16]. Cross-Cut Test uses pressure-sensitive tape spread over cuts and rapidly pulled off [17]. Adhesion is rated by inspecting the cut area for coating removal relative to classifications 0B through 5B [16]. Cross-cut adhesion test evaluation ranges from 0B representing complete detachment to 5B representing no detachment [18]. ASTM D3359 Standard Test Method for Measuring Adhesion by Tape Test was codified in 1974 [19]. ASTM D3359 is a standard test method for measuring adhesion by tape test [20]. Crosshatch adhesion testing follows ASTM D3359 standard with grid cuts through coating [21]. ASTM D3359 is the standard method for rating adhesion by tape test [22]. Peel testing represents a third category, measuring the force required to strip a flexible coating from a substrate [23]. Peel tests measure adhesive strength by pulling one material away from another at a specific angle and speed [24]. This method is suitable for designs where bonded edges experience prying or bending [25]. Peel and adhesion strength testing measures bond strength when force attempts separation [25]. Comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance [26]. The peel test is a method used in materials science to measure the bond strength between two materials [27]. Peel strength is usually reported in units of force per width such as N/mm or lb/in [27]. Peel testing methods include T-peel and 90-degree peel tests standardized under ASTM D1876 and D6862 [28]. Standard testing procedures evaluate peel force under constant temperature 23±1°C and 50±5% RH [29]. Peel test values represent an average between 5 and the reported value of peel for each of two tests. T-peel testing was performed according to BS 5350-C12:1994/ISO 11339:1993 standard on aluminium alloy [30]. Chinese national standard GB/T 2790-1995 specifies the 180° peel strength test method steps and requirements [31]. ISO 8510-2 covers 180-degree peel test for flexible-bonded-to-rigid test specimen assembly [24]. The T shaped peel test ASTM D1876 is most widely used for thin-gauge metal adherends [26]. Table 1 summarizes the comparative frameworks for these primary adhesion testing methods, highlighting standard designations and operational limits. | Test Method | Primary Standard (ASTM/ISO) | Substrate Suitability | Measurement Type | Thickness Limit | |:--- |:--- |:--- |:--- |:--- | | Pull-Off | ASTM D4541, ISO 4624 | Metal, Concrete, Rigid | Quantitative (MPa) | N/A [11] | | Cross-Cut | ASTM D3359, ISO 2409 | Metal, Plastic, Rigid | Qualitative (0B-5B) | 250 µm [32] | | Peel | ASTM D1876, ISO 11339 | Flexible-to-Rigid | Quantitative (N/mm) | N/A [28] | | Knife/Scrape | ASTM D6677 | Hard Coatings | Qualitative/Force | N/A [22] | Thickness constraints significantly influence method selection for polymer coatings. The Cross-Cut Test is limited by coating thickness with a maximum of 250 micrometers [32]. Cross-cut test is applicable for layer thicknesses up to about 250 µm [33]. This limitation renders the cross-cut method unsuitable for thick urethane or epoxy linings often exceeding this threshold. The test method covers determination of practical adhesion strength of hard ceramic coatings on metal and ceramic substrates [34]. Test method is commonly used for coating thicknesses in the range of 0.1 to 30 micrometers [34]. Adhesion pull-off strength testing determines the overall quality and integrity of an installed coating system [35]. Cross hatch tape testing is intended for laboratory use on coatings under 5 mils thickness [22]. Scrape test adhesion is measured in kilograms of force required for removal [36]. ASTM D6677 is the standard method for evaluating adhesion by knife [22]. Cross-cut test is especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic [33]. The crosscut adhesion test is a standard ASTM or ISO procedure for dry coatings [37]. Conflicting evidence exists regarding the fundamental capabilities of these tests and specific procedural dimensions. One source states the pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface [4]. However, conflicting evidence indicates the test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate [34]. This discrepancy suggests a distinction between practical field adhesion and theoretical interfacial strength. Regarding cut patterns, evidence states cross-cut test uses a tool to cut a right-angle lattice pattern through the coating to the substrate [1]. Other evidence indicates X-cut tape test uses a sharp tool to make an X-shaped cut through the coating to substrate [4]. ASTM D3359 accommodates both via Method A (X-Cut) and Method B (Cross-Cut Tape Test), with Method B preferred for laboratory or controlled environments [12]. Test Method A (X-Cut Tape Test) is designed primarily for field use with X-shaped cut in coating. Cross-Hatch Test is typically used on witness samples of the same substrate material [38]. Procedural variations also appear in peel test specimen dimensions. Evidence indicates DOWSIL ALLGUARD coating adhesion test uses cheesecloth strip 1 inch by 12 inches embedded in wet coating [39]. In conflict, another source states peel test uses 1 inch wide by 6 inch long fabric strip embedded in coating base layer [40]. These variations likely reflect specific manufacturer protocols versus general standardized methods. ISO 4624 is referenced as a global standard for pull-off adhesion testing compliance [5]. Traditional adhesion tests like ISO/JIS cross-hatch and ASTM tape tests only evaluate final adhesion strength without explaining adhesion mechanisms [41]. Surface free energy and adhesion parameter measurements are proposed as complementary methods to traditional cross-cut tape tests [41]. Adhesion testing mechanics are complicated when polymer adhesive and metal substrate have different elastic moduli [42]. This complexity is particularly relevant for silicone and urethane coatings which exhibit different elastic properties compared to epoxy systems. Peel and pull-off data should not be compared since testing mechanisms differ [14]. Four common methods exist for testing adhesion of protective coatings and linings per ASTM standards [14]. Common adhesion measuring techniques are performed using a knife or a pull-off adhesion tester. Specific material considerations emerge within the evidence regarding epoxy and silicone systems. Results showed good agreement between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel tests [43]. This suggests environmental conditioning impacts epoxy coating performance assessment. The majority of bond systems underwent tensile adhesion strength testing using a button-to-plate configuration [44]. Silicone contamination impacts bond performance, necessitating rigorous testing protocols [44]. Video How to Perform an Adhesion Test Patch provides more information on roof coating adhesion tests [45]. This indicates field verification is critical for urethane and silicone roof applications. Adhesion test must be conducted prior to estimating coating restoration project per Inland adhesion testing procedures [46]. Adhesive performance evaluation after testing includes checking bonding strength, color stability, surface integrity, and chemical stability [47]. Tensile or shear tests measure strength between bonded materials for adhesives [48]. The study objective was to identify an optimal adhesive for the pull-off test providing higher adhesion [9]. Pull-off method provides values for peel stress allowing quick determination and comparison of coating adhesion [11]. The proposed adhesion testing method uses direct pull-off strength measurements at various corrosion stages [49]. Field adhesion test recommended when laboratory test cannot be performed on-site [50]. Field adhesion test also called hand pull test verifies proper sealant installation. There is no universally accepted test for conformal coating adhesion [38]. SAE J400 standard outlines specialized coating adhesion test method using Instron testing machine [13]. Latter two ASTM methods evaluate tensile or pull-off strength of coating [14]. The test method was originally developed for ductile coatings applied to metal substrates [16]. Site adhesion testing must be verified according to criteria on page 35 to confirm proper surface preparation [51]. Nitto Denko employs adhesive pull strength evaluation using JIS Z0237 and ASTM standards [52]. Mechanical analysis services include testing for adhesive strength, tensile strength, yield stress. All structural applications must be tested by the Dow test lab for surface preparation recommendations. A similar testing program is needed for concrete coatings to enable laboratory verification. Adhesive testing products include epoxy glue, polyurethane glue, and organic silicone among many others [47]. Tensile bulk tests were performed using long dogbone specimens to characterize adhesives at room temperature [53]. Higher yield strength values obtained in compression test than in tensile test for all adhesives. Study of size dependence of bulk specimen was carried out to evaluate scale factors in adhesive testing. NPL and partners developed a tape peel test applicable to PCB or component surfaces [54]. ATS offers ASTM paint adhesion testing to determine adhesive performance according to industry standards. Peel tests involve pulling flexible material from a surface at defined angles [48]. **Key Findings** * Pull-off methods (ASTM D4541, ISO 4624) provide quantitative tensile strength data suitable for thick epoxy and urethane systems, whereas cross-cut tests (ASTM D3359) offer qualitative ratings limited to coatings under 250 µm [32]. * Conflicting evidence exists regarding whether pull-off tests measure fundamental adhesion strength or practical bond integrity, indicating a need for method selection based on specific performance criteria [4][34]. * Peel testing standards (ASTM D1876, ISO 11339) quantify force per width (N/mm) for flexible-to-rigid bonds, essential for silicone and urethane membrane evaluations [28][27]. * ASTM D3359 encompasses both X-cut and lattice patterns, with the lattice method preferred for laboratory precision and the X-cut for field applications [1]. * Environmental conditions and substrate elastic moduli significantly influence test outcomes, requiring controlled temperature and humidity during peel and tensile assessments [42][29]. ! Specifically, *Figure 2: Bar chart displaying document engagement metrics such as views and ratings. | Published Year | 2019 | year |. *Table 1: Key Timeline Events for ASTM C1624*. ## Pull-Off Testing Mechanical Application and Procedures Pull-off testing represents a primary quantitative methodology for evaluating bond strength, distinct from qualitative assessments found in cross-cut or peel configurations. This perpendicular application differentiates pull-off mechanics from peel tests, which measure the force required to strip a flexible coating from a substrate at specific angles [23]. In particular, for polymer coatings such as epoxy, urethane, and silicone, the choice of mechanical application directly influences the interpretability of adhesion strength regarding regulatory compliance and field performance. The core mechanism utilizes a fixture, often referred to as a dolly, which is adhesively bonded to the cured coating surface [36]. Once bonded, the tester exerts a pulling force until failure occurs, providing a quantitative indication of bond tensile strength. This mechanical setup allows for the direct determination of adhesion strength at the metallisation or polymer interface from the substrate [67]. The mechanical distinction is critical for the research question comparing methods across materials; pull-off tests are typical for industrial coatings including polymer coatings and epoxy resins, whereas cross-cut tests are especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic. Standardization governs the mechanical procedures to ensure comparability across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. The evolution of these standards reflects the industry's shift toward quantitative evaluation methods. The following table outlines key standards and their establishment or revision dates relevant to mechanical adhesion testing procedures. | Standard Designation | Method Type | Establishment/Revision Year | Evaluation Type |. | ASTM D3359 | Tape Test | 1974 [19] | Qualitative |. | JIS K 5600-5-6 | Cross-Cut Test | 1999 | Qualitative |. | JIS K 5600-5-7 | Pull-Off Method | 2014 | Quantitative |. | ISO 4624 | Pull-Off Adhesion | 2023 [9] | Quantitative [9] |. The temporal progression in the table indicates a standardization trend toward quantitative tensile testing methods like ISO 4624 and JIS K 5600-5-7. However, conflicting evidence exists regarding the specific scope and dates of Japanese Industrial Standards. One source specifies JIS K 5600-5-7: 2014 for the Pull-Off Method as a coating tensile adhesion test for quantitative evaluation. Another source indicates JIS K 5600-5-6: 1999 specifies the Cross-Cut Test method for coating adhesion evaluation under Japanese Industrial Standards. While the conflict detector flags this as a contradiction, the evidence suggests these represent different parts of the same standard framework addressing different mechanical methods rather than mutually exclusive facts. The 1999 date applies to cross-cut mechanics, while the 2014 date applies to pull-off mechanics, highlighting the divergent procedural requirements within the same regulatory body. Similarly, distinctions in ASTM standards clarify the mechanical application differences. These standards support different mechanical actions; the tape test relies on adhesive removal force, while the knife test evaluates resistance to scraping. Quantitative outcomes from pull-off mechanics offer specific metrics for epoxy and silicone materials. Specific material data shows a button pull-off test measured exterior paint average bond strength at 7.27 MPa (1050 psi) [68]. These numerical values underscore the quantitative advantage of pull-off mechanics over the 0B to 5B classification range found in cross-cut adhesion test evaluation [18]. For silicone rubber and epoxy resin interfaces, pull-off tests were applied to estimate adhesion effect, demonstrating utility across these specific polymer types [69]. Mechanical application procedures also dictate sample validity. This requirement ensures that the measured force reflects coating adhesion rather than adhesive failure between the dolly and the coating. This mechanical difference implies that peel tests may better simulate edge failures in flexible silicone or urethane coatings, while pull-off tests better simulate normal stress failures in rigid epoxy systems. Environmental and procedural factors further influence mechanical test outcomes. These factors are critical when comparing methods across materials, as urethane and silicone adhesives may cure differently under varying conditions. In particular, relying solely on one mechanical method, such as the cross-cut test which uses pressure-sensitive tape spread over cuts and rapidly pulled off, may not capture the full performance profile of complex polymer systems [17]. Field application mechanics differ from laboratory settings. A simple qualitative pull test can be performed by hand by pulling silicone with reasonable strength. Nevertheless, this mismatch affects stress distribution during pull-off or peel procedures. Regulatory compliance often dictates the mechanical method selected. Note that while these standards evaluate peel strength, the mechanical action is tensile pull-off, distinguishing it from flexible peel testing defined by ISO 8510-2 which covers 180-degree peel test for flexible-bonded-to-rigid test specimen assembly. These distinctions are vital for the research question, as epoxy coatings typically require tensile pull-off (ASTM D4541), while flexible silicone or urethane membranes may require peel configurations (ASTM D1876). Cross-cut test classification based on percentage of area affected by coating removal provides a visual metric distinct from force measurements [68]. This prohibition underscores the necessity of method-specific benchmarks when comparing epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. * Pull-off testing applies perpendicular tensile force via a dolly to provide quantitative adhesion strength values in MPa or psi, distinguishing it from qualitative cross-cut methods. * Standardization trends show a progression from qualitative tape tests (ASTM D3359, 1974) to quantitative pull-off methods (ISO 4624, 2023), influencing regulatory compliance for polymer coatings. * Mechanical suitability varies by material, with pull-off tests typical for epoxy resins and rigid substrates, while peel tests are suitable for flexible coatings experiencing prying or bending [25]. * Peel and pull-off data mechanisms differ significantly, preventing direct comparison of strength values across these testing methodologies. ! ASTM D4541 Standard Test Method for Pull-Off Strength requires portable adhesion tester apparatus. The ASTM D4541 Pull-Off Test measures tensile pull-off force required to detach dolly adhesively bonded to coating from substrate. While peel tests address specific stress modes, pull-off tests address normal stress. ASTM D4541 pull-off test is a fundamental standard for measuring adhesive strength on metallic substrates [70]. For epoxy adhesives, toughened epoxy adhesive Bondmaster E3334 was tested with ATBN reactive liquid polymer toughening agent [30]. Method A results assessed on 0A-5A scale where 5A indicates no peeling and 0A indicates complete removal. These qualitative scales contrast with the continuous numerical data from pull-off tests. The FAS-CeO2/PDMS coating achieved cross-cut adhesion classification of level 2 corresponding to 4B rating. This specific data point illustrates how qualitative ratings map to material performance. However, independent laboratories have noted differences of up to 50 percent in tape adhesion variability over time. This distinction is vital for the research question comparing adhesion testing methods across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials, as the mechanical loading mode significantly influences failure modes. However, the configuration of the peel test dictates the stress distribution at the peel front. Different peel test types include 90-degree peel, 180-degree peel, T-peel, floating roller peel, loop tack strength test, and Double Cantilever Beam test. In contrast, the 180° peel strength test involves bonding two materials, stripping part of the material, and applying force at a 180° angle [31]. For flexible materials bonded to each other, the T-Peel Test is designed to measure the force required to separate two flexible materials that have been bonded. These variations allow analysts to select the geometry that best mimics the service conditions of the polymer coating. **Table 1: Comparison of Peel Test Configurations and Standards**. | Configuration | Primary Standard | Substrate Suitability | Measurement Unit |. | 90-Degree Peel | ASTM D6862 | Semi-rigid or rigid | Force per width |. | 180-Degree Peel | ISO 8510-2 | Flexible-bonded-to-rigid | N/mm or lb/in |. | T-Peel | ASTM D1876 | Thin-gauge metal adherends | Force per width |. | Cross-Cut | ISO 2409 | Thin coatings on rigid substrates [21] | Classification 0B-5B |. This granularity allows for more nuanced performance tracking than the binary pass/fail nature of some tape tests. This disagreement highlights a critical trade-off in adhesion testing: practical bond strength evaluation versus theoretical interfacial energy measurement. The position that tests measure practical bond strength has stronger support across the evidence base, as multiple sources define these methods by their ability to quantify resistance to separation. The pull-off device provides values for the peel stress, which not only allows a quick determination of the adhesion of the coating to the substrate, but also makes it easier to compare the adhesion of several coatings to each other. Testing speed is another variable influencing peel test outcomes. In one study, peel speed was varied from 0.5 to 1.5mm/s to test effects on three coatings [54]. Results showed little dependence on peel speed, but low adhesion strength of silicones was apparent. This shows that while speed is a controlled parameter in standards, it may not be the dominant factor for certain polymer chemistries like silicone. Peel force is applied to an edge of the adherend and substrate and determined from average value. Material suitability varies significantly across testing methods. This makes peel testing more appropriate for flexible polymer coatings such as urethanes and silicones used in roofing or conformal applications, whereas cross-cut methods favor rigid epoxy systems on metal. Standards compliance ensures reproducibility across these variations. Specifically, the existence of multiple standards for similar measurements indicates that regulatory compliance depends on the specific industry sector, such as automotive versus construction. This global proliferation of standards necessitates careful selection based on geographic regulatory requirements. Sample preparation introduces further variables affecting data integrity. However, conflicting evidence suggests a coating adhesion test requires pulling cheesecloth at 180 degree angle at slow steady rate without specifying dimensions. Environmental control is therefore critical when comparing data across different laboratories or field conditions. Roof coating must cure minimum 72 hours before conducting peel adhesion test. This curing requirement is critical for urethane and silicone roof coatings where full chemical crosslinking takes time. Performance scoring differs between quantitative and qualitative methods. Tape test procedures involve applying pressure-sensitive adhesive tape on cross-hatched coating area and removing quickly. This high variability supports the use of peel tests for critical applications requiring precise numerical data. However, paint and tape adhesion tests are useful for screening surface prep and cure on coatings. Thus, tape methods serve a quality control function while peel and pull-off methods serve performance validation functions. Material specific performance also dictates method selection. Adhesive formulations are designed to provide excellent adhesion and high shear and peel strength [72]. Silicone materials exhibited low adhesion strength in peel contexts. This aligns with the known lower surface energy of silicones compared to epoxies and urethanes. Consequently, testing silicone coatings may require more sensitive peel configurations or surface energy measurements as complementary methods. Thickness constraints further limit method applicability. This narrow range excludes many thick polymer linings used in industrial settings. This failure mode requirement ensures that cohesive failure within the coating is distinguished from adhesive failure at the interface. Regulatory frameworks reinforce these methodological distinctions. Peel force peaks occur at 60° and 180° peel angles where more peeling force is borne by tape backing [29]. This mechanical phenomenon shows that 90-degree configurations might offer a more direct measure of interfacial adhesion by minimizing backing deformation effects. This division between field and laboratory methods underscores the trade-off between precision and portability. Cross-cut tape peel test was used to evaluate the adhesion property of polymeric coatings by cutting a lattice pattern of small squares [73]. Surface contaminants and preparation impact all methods. Using a calibrated knife reduced subjectivity found in other adhesion tests. However, Cross-cut tape peel test was used to evaluate the adhesion property of polymeric coatings by cutting a lattice pattern of small squares [73]. Consistency in cut depth is therefore paramount for reproducible cross-cut results. Scrape Test procedure applies adhesive to smooth substrate at uniform thickness then uses rounded stylus [17]. * Peel test configurations (90-degree, 180-degree, T-peel) provide quantitative force per width data (N/mm or lb/in) suitable for flexible polymer coatings, unlike the qualitative 0B-5B classification of cross-cut tests. * Peel speed variations (0.5 to 1.5mm/s) showed little dependence in silicone materials, suggesting chemistry may influence speed sensitivity more than test geometry. * Methodological conflicts exist regarding whether tests measure fundamental adhesion strength or practical bond resistance, with evidence favoring the latter for engineering applications. * Regulatory compliance requires adherence to specific standards such as ASTM D1876 for T-peel or ISO 2409 for cross-cut, depending on substrate type and geographic jurisdiction. ## Peel Testing Standards ASTM D903 and ISO11339 Compliance Peel testing standards such as ASTM D903 and ISO 11339 provide the structural basis for quantifying adhesive strength in flexible coating systems, distinguishing them from qualitative alternatives. .codex\I-f4-004\codex_diff_audit.txt:2733:.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_1\codex_stderr.log:2987:outputs\polaris_graph\GEMINI_E2E_20260316_092353.json:44586:  "draft_report": "# Comparative Analysis of Adhesion Testing Methods for Polymer Coatings: Methodologies, Performance, and Regulatory Compliance\n\n## Abstract\n\nThis report evaluates adhesion testing methodologies for polymer coatings, specifically comparing pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. The scope encompasses methodological standards, failure analysis, and environmental impacts on bond integrity, drawing from established ASTM and ISO protocols. Exclusions involve non-polymer substrates, dynamic fatigue testing not covered by static adhesion standards, and proprietary industrial methods lacking public documentation. Evidence limitations include variability in industry-specific documentation and potential gaps in recent silicone-specific comparative data regarding long-term exposure. The analysis synthesizes regulatory requirements with practical performance metrics to guide method selection. Findings highlight critical distinctions in failure mode classification and the influence of surface preparation on test validity. Regulatory compliance and warranty documentation requirements are also addressed to ensure operational reliability. This structured comparison aims to reduce testing ambiguity and improve consistency in coating performance evaluation across diverse service conditions. Furthermore, economic implications of testing choices are considered to balance accuracy with operational costs. The report concludes with strategic recommendations for optimizing test selection based on material properties. Overall, the study provides a comprehensive framework for stakeholders seeking to validate coating durability and adherence quality in complex engineering applications.\n\n## Standardized Test Method Definitions and ASTM ISO Frameworks\n\nAdhesion testing frameworks for polymer coatings are governed by a complex array of ASTM and ISO standards that define methodological rigor and compliance thresholds [CITE:ev_057ad7d7fadf39c2]. These standardized protocols establish the baseline for comparing pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials [CITE:ev_2b7ca35715831266]. The regulatory landscape distinguishes between quantitative tensile measurements and qualitative visual assessments, each serving specific diagnostic purposes within the coating lifecycle [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. Pull-off adhesion testing applies tensile force perpendicular to the substrate to measure coating resistance to separation [CITE:ev_057ad7d7fadf39c2]. This method is utilized to gage adhesive strength of the coating once applied and cured as per manufacturer's guidelines [CITE:ev_2b7ca35715831266]. The pull-off adhesion tester measures adhesion of coatings to metal, wood, concrete, and rigid substrates, providing versatility across material classes [CITE:ev_0a2f7e8a1aa2c852]. Pull-off test data provides quantitative indication of bond tensile strength [CITE:ev_448284e198da9b99]. Industrial-grade adhesives typically have tensile strength ranging from 10 MPa to over 60 MPa depending on formulation [CITE:ev_a85f361a2bdff124]. Hydroxyapatite coatings showed repeatable adhesion strength of 3.4 \u00b1 1.0 MPa across both ASTM test methods [CITE:ev_5bd33c7a02d9cd28]. ASTM C633-01 test uses 25 mm diameter cylindrical blocks with 38 mm length for adhesion testing [CITE:ev_e9f86ec169988ea]. ASTM D4541 and BS EN ISO 4624 describe pull-off adhesion testing standards for protective coatings [CITE:ev_befc6654b3c8f5ad]. The pull-off test is standardized by ISO 4624:2023 and is widely used to quantify adhesion [CITE:ev_7267f03f92de9267]. ASTM D7234 defines the standard test method for pull-off adhesion strength on concrete using portable testers [CITE:ev_3a7d481c74614432]. Pull-off testing follows ISO 4624, ASTM D4541, and D7234 standards for evaluating coating peel strength [CITE:ev_0ffc84130522e5ce]. Valid pull-off test requires coating to cover at least 50% of stamp surface after detachment [CITE:ev_d0994f38fa60bebe].\n\nThe Cross-Cut Test provides qualitative evaluation while the Pull-Off Method provides quantitative evaluation of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. This distinction is critical when selecting methods for epoxy, urethane, or silicone systems, as quantitative data may be required for structural warranty validation. Cross-Cut Test classifies adhesion results into six levels according to the degree of peeling after tape removal [CITE:ev_cd6ffc422440dd18]. The procedure involves making lattice-pattern cuts through the coating to the substrate, applying adhesive tape, and peeling in one motion [CITE:ev_4add28fe26ddd5e3]. ASTM D3359 outlines standardized test methods for evaluating adhesion of coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape [CITE:ev_815bc543dbac8f04]. This standard is widely recognized for evaluating coating adhesion on metal substrates using the tape test method [CITE:ev_59cf9ed236eb49bd]. ISO 2409 specifies the cross-cut test method for evaluating adhesion of coatings on sheet metal parts [CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2]. ASTM D3359 defines adhesion as molecular attraction and mechanical bonds between coating and substrate [CITE:ev_a930d76424157b92]. ASTM D3359 procedures assess the adhesion of coating films specifically on metallic substrates [CITE:ev_949116df55b626a8]. The tape test involves applying and removing pressure-sensitive tape over cuts made in the film [CITE:ev_b737d3f89fc71af0]. The tape test is qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesion [CITE:ev_389d441cc3fe3ba4]. Cross-Cut Test uses pressure-sensitive tape spread over cuts and rapidly pulled off [CITE:ev_25929b033d8bd227]. Adhesion is rated by inspecting the cut area for coating removal relative to classifications 0B through 5B [CITE:ev_d23f5ac9162865a3]. Cross-cut adhesion test evaluation ranges from 0B representing complete detachment to 5B representing no detachment [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3]. ASTM D3359 Standard Test Method for Measuring Adhesion by Tape Test was codified in 1974 [CITE:ev_cee97c994fefc9b1]. ASTM D3359 is a standard test method for measuring adhesion by tape test [CITE:ev_23c66a42fadbecc5]. Crosshatch adhesion testing follows ASTM D3359 standard with grid cuts through coating [CITE:ev_2365cdb310fc8adf]. ASTM D3359 is the standard method for rating adhesion by tape test [CITE:ev_d2c3f9dace34682e].\n\nPeel testing represents a third category, measuring the force required to strip a flexible coating from a substrate [CITE:ev_6e9c26b6c7722bbe]. Peel tests measure adhesive strength by pulling one material away from another at a specific angle and speed [CITE:ev_f0f1594bc5d9dd20]. This method is suitable for designs where bonded edges experience prying or bending [CITE:ev_1be768df70c46a07]. Peel and adhesion strength testing measures bond strength when force attempts separation [CITE:ev_27f9ac91a5f22e9b]. Comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd]. The peel test is a method used in materials science to measure the bond strength between two materials [CITE:ev_6bcf28f6b901e894]. Peel strength is usually reported in units of force per width such as N/mm or lb/in [CITE:ev_44fea46094d83d2a]. Peel testing methods include T-peel and 90-degree peel tests standardized under ASTM D1876 and D6862 [CITE:ev_16c2d5e21aa178ca]. Standard testing procedures evaluate peel force under constant temperature 23\u00b11\u00b0C and 50\u00b15% RH [CITE:ev_4420310a01914e20]. Peel test values represent an average between 5 and 35mm of peel for each of two tests [CITE:ev_e84426d0da5ec986]. T-peel testing was performed according to BS 5350-C12:1994/ISO 11339:1993 standard on aluminium alloy [CITE:ev_f93ca08a9dc0966f]. Chinese national standard GB/T 2790-1995 specifies the 180\u00b0 peel strength test method steps and requirements [CITE:ev_4ec63e8773cfcd3e]. ISO 8510-2 covers 180-degree peel test for flexible-bonded-to-rigid test specimen assembly [CITE:ev_75d316de9669a24a]. The T shaped peel test ASTM D1876 is most widely used for thin-gauge metal adherends [CITE:ev_e3a5ffd4420e583a].\n\nTable 1 summarizes the comparative frameworks for these primary adhesion testing methods, highlighting standard designations and operational limits.\n\n| Test Method | Primary Standard (ASTM/ISO) | Substrate Suitability | Measurement Type | Thickness Limit |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| Pull-Off | ASTM D4541, ISO 4624 | Metal, Concrete, Rigid | Quantitative (MPa) | N/A [CITE:ev_0ffc84130522e5ce] |\n| Cross-Cut | ASTM D3359, ISO 2409 | Metal, Plastic, Rigid | Qualitative (0B-5B) | 250 \u00b5m [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207] |\n| Peel | ASTM D1876, ISO 11339 | Flexible-to-Rigid | Quantitative (N/mm) | N/A [CITE:ev_16c2d5e21aa178ca] |\n| Knife/Scrape | ASTM D6677 | Hard Coatings | Qualitative/Force | N/A [CITE:ev_3f498ca240e7a532] |\n\nThickness constraints significantly influence method selection for polymer coatings. The Cross-Cut Test is limited by coating thickness with a maximum of 250 micrometers [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207]. Cross-cut test is applicable for layer thicknesses up to about 250 \u00b5m [CITE:ev_df003953c7f7a2d0]. This limitation renders the cross-cut method unsuitable for thick urethane or epoxy linings often exceeding this threshold. Conversely, the test method covers determination of practical adhesion strength of hard ceramic coatings on metal and ceramic substrates [CITE:ev_f6c7a8ecd3f37045]. Test method is commonly used for coating thicknesses in the range of 0.1 to 30 micrometers [CITE:ev_8858c4a9dba538b2]. Adhesion pull-off strength testing determines the overall quality and integrity of an installed coating system [CITE:ev_c357968c8f3b130a]. Cross hatch tape testing is intended for laboratory use on coatings under 5 mils thickness [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea]. Scrape test adhesion is measured in kilograms of force required for removal [CITE:ev_73f6c6e661c92e30]. ASTM D6677 is the standard method for evaluating adhesion by knife [CITE:ev_3f498ca240e7a532]. Cross-cut test is especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic [CITE:ev_bb2ddf4927283db1]. The crosscut adhesion test is a standard ASTM or ISO procedure for dry coatings [CITE:ev_49557422c8fc215a].\n\nConflicting evidence exists regarding the fundamental capabilities of these tests and specific procedural dimensions. One source states the pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00]. However, conflicting evidence indicates the test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. This discrepancy suggests a distinction between practical field adhesion and theoretical interfacial strength. Regarding cut patterns, evidence states cross-cut test uses a tool to cut a right-angle lattice pattern through the coating to the substrate [CITE:ev_183b421be46e5e8b]. Conversely, other evidence indicates X-cut tape test uses a sharp tool to make an X-shaped cut through the coating to substrate [CITE:ev_377599bde46462b1]. ASTM D3359 accommodates both via Method A (X-Cut) and Method B (Cross-Cut Tape Test), with Method B preferred for laboratory or controlled environments [CITE:ev_a673f1e5d40af5bb]. Test Method A (X-Cut Tape Test) is designed primarily for field use with X-shaped cut in coating [CITE:ev_e46b804dd9b8226]. Cross-Hatch Test is typically used on witness samples of the same substrate material [CITE:ev_26a79c112c1e2bd7].\n\nProcedural variations also appear in peel test specimen dimensions. Evidence indicates DOWSIL ALLGUARD coating adhesion test uses cheesecloth strip 1 inch by 12 inches embedded in wet coating [CITE:ev_234e921351755d24]. In conflict, another source states peel test uses 1 inch wide by 6 inch long fabric strip embedded in coating base layer [CITE:ev_14c32495c2f3b455]. These variations likely reflect specific manufacturer protocols versus general standardized methods. ISO 4624 is referenced as a global standard for pull-off adhesion testing compliance [CITE:ev_a9eaff2972823a42]. Traditional adhesion tests like ISO/JIS cross-hatch and ASTM tape tests only evaluate final adhesion strength without explaining adhesion mechanisms [CITE:ev_60e99e00ac021116]. Surface free energy and adhesion parameter measurements are proposed as complementary methods to traditional cross-cut tape tests [CITE:ev_d77bb9a950ab7132]. Adhesion testing mechanics are complicated when polymer adhesive and metal substrate have different elastic moduli [CITE:ev_2fc7d6f56363052a]. This complexity is particularly relevant for silicone and urethane coatings which exhibit different elastic properties compared to epoxy systems. Peel and pull-off data should not be compared since testing mechanisms differ [CITE:ev_aabe5f967b113f45]. Four common methods exist for testing adhesion of protective coatings and linings per ASTM standards [CITE:ev_c53e1e15e6c509b0]. Common adhesion measuring techniques are performed using a knife or a pull-off adhesion tester [CITE:ev_ac710f7b1e8e52c6].\n\nSpecific material considerations emerge within the evidence regarding epoxy and silicone systems. Results showed good agreement between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel tests [CITE:ev_6c07ce38730c09f8]. This suggests environmental conditioning impacts epoxy coating performance assessment. The majority of bond systems underwent tensile adhesion strength testing using a button-to-plate configuration [CITE:ev_e552dd2b19ec130e]. Silicone contamination impacts bond performance, necessitating rigorous testing protocols [CITE:ev_e552dd2b19ec130e]. Video How to Perform an Adhesion Test Patch provides more information on roof coating adhesion tests [CITE:ev_537eee4252bc6670]. This indicates field verification is critical for urethane and silicone roof applications. Adhesion test must be conducted prior to estimating coating restoration project per Inland adhesion testing procedures [CITE:ev_61c41c390dad6a70]. Adhesive performance evaluation after testing includes checking bonding strength, color stability, surface integrity, and chemical stability [CITE:ev_eecd425930fc2a46]. Tensile or shear tests measure strength between bonded materials for adhesives [CITE:ev_2248a1dd659c4419]. The study objective was to identify an optimal adhesive for the pull-off test providing higher adhesion [CITE:ev_f8a2b17c15a40670]. Pull-off method provides values for peel stress allowing quick determination and comparison of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_03d5cf9642fafd22]. The proposed adhesion testing method uses direct pull-off strength measurements at various corrosion stages [CITE:ev_6592a406ace27776]. Field adhesion test recommended when laboratory test cannot be performed on-site [CITE:ev_f569492a9905b1d9]. Field adhesion test also called hand pull test verifies proper sealant installation [CITE:ev_7658ca3c2e89c2e9]. There is no universally accepted test for conformal coating adhesion [CITE:ev_c30e40e01bb9b77c]. SAE J400 standard outlines specialized coating adhesion test method using Instron testing machine [CITE:ev_b26b994900339f4e]. Latter two ASTM methods evaluate tensile or pull-off strength of coating [CITE:ev_d7edcf2ffc730737]. The test method was originally developed for ductile coatings applied to metal substrates [CITE:ev_5b8a012a381ea628]. Site adhesion testing must be verified according to criteria on page 35 to confirm proper surface preparation [CITE:ev_97840fd9469c02ca]. Nitto Denko employs adhesive pull strength evaluation using JIS Z0237 and ASTM standards [CITE:ev_d063340009e4394c]. Mechanical analysis services include testing for adhesive strength, tensile strength, yield stress [CITE:ev_1f32724ed272e28]. All structural applications must be tested by the Dow test lab for surface preparation recommendations [CITE:ev_24d4477c668a302]. A similar testing program is needed for concrete coatings to enable laboratory verification [CITE:ev_49d5639552dc7c2]. Adhesive testing products include epoxy glue, polyurethane glue, and organic silicone among many others [CITE:ev_5b179a7386ac92ce]. Tensile bulk tests were performed using long dogbone specimens to characterize adhesives at room temperature [CITE:ev_59bf06dce7ffc5c5]. Higher yield strength values obtained in compression test than in tensile test for all adhesives [CITE:ev_73023757450301de]. Study of size dependence of bulk specimen was carried out to evaluate scale factors in adhesive testing [CITE:ev_26d2fc42825fb10]. NPL and partners developed a tape peel test applicable to PCB or component surfaces [CITE:ev_7416ad3c2e89c2e9]. ATS offers ASTM paint adhesion testing to determine adhesive performance according to industry standards [CITE:ev_22e6e69ce64f2b]. Peel tests involve pulling flexible material from a surface at defined angles [CITE:ev_f06a21a7fab15411].\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n*   Pull-off methods (ASTM D4541, ISO 4624) provide quantitative tensile strength data suitable for thick epoxy and urethane systems, whereas cross-cut tests (ASTM D3359) offer qualitative ratings limited to coatings under 250 \u00b5m import { expect, test } from "@playwright/test";

const SURFACES: { id: number; surface: string }[] = [
  { id: 18, surface: "table" },
  { id: 19, surface: "summary_bullet" },
  { id: 20, surface: "limitation" },
  { id: 21, surface: "caption" },
  { id: 22, surface: "heading" },
];

test("Report header shows assertion-surface legend", async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto("/sentence_hover_test");
  await expect(page.getByTestId("assertion-surface-legend")).toBeVisible();
  await expect(page.getByTestId("assertion-surface-legend")).toContainText(
    "Prose",
  );
  await expect(page.getByTestId("assertion-surface-legend")).toContainText(
    "Heading",
  );
});

for (const { id, surface } of SURFACES) {
  test(`${surface} sentence is clickable and opens Inspector`, async ({
    page,
  }) => {
    await page.goto("/sentence_hover_test");
    const row = page.locator(`[data-sentence-id="sec_x:${id}"]`);
    await expect(row.getByTestId(`surface-badge-${surface}`)).toBeVisible();
    await row.click();
    await expect(page.getByTestId("sentence-inspector-sheet")).toBeVisible({
      timeout: 500,
    });
  });
}

import { expect, test } from "@playwright/test";

test("Inspector renders synthesis-claim badge for is_synthesis_claim=true", async ({
  page,
}) => {
  await page.goto("/sentence_hover_test");
  await page.locator('[data-sentence-id="sec_x:15"]').click();

  await expect(page.getByTestId("sentence-inspector-sheet")).toBeVisible({
    timeout: 500,
  });
  await expect(page.getByTestId("inspector-synthesis-claim")).toBeVisible();
});

test("Inspector does NOT render synthesis-claim badge for non-synthesis sentence", async ({
  page,
}) => {
  await page.goto("/sentence_hover_test");
  await page.locator('[data-sentence-id="sec_x:5"]').click();

  await expect(page.getByTestId("sentence-inspector-sheet")).toBeVisible({
    timeout: 500,
  });
  await expect(page.getByTestId("inspector-synthesis-claim")).toHaveCount(0);
});

# POLARIS Operational Charter v1

**Authority:** aldrinor only, GPG/SSH-signed commits to polaris-controls
**Effective:** 2026-05-04
**Pinned in POLARIS:** docs/session_pin.txt → charter_sha256
**This document is the highest-authority operational rulebook. Conflicts resolve in favor of this document.**

---

## The 8 Hard Rules

### 1. Roles

- **Claude Code** is architect + reviewer ONLY.
- **Codex CLI** is executor ONLY.
- **User (aldrinor)** is spec owner + merge gate.
- No agent has signing authority on any commit to POLARIS main.
- Out-of-order pipeline execution = blocked by Trusted App server-side.

### 2. Slices

Work proceeds in slices. **One slice active at a time.**

- Slice spec lives at `polaris-controls/slices/<active_slice>.md`
- Claude reads the active slice spec at session start
- Claude does nothing outside its declared scope
- Slice scope = files explicitly listed; everything else is out of bounds

### 3. PR Size Cap

**200 LOC per pull request. Hard ceiling.**

- Trusted App rejects PRs exceeding 200 net additions
- Larger work decomposes into multiple slices, not larger PRs
- No "this PR is special" exceptions

### 4. Golden Tests as Fitness Function

Every slice has golden-master tests in `polaris-controls/golden/<slice_id>/`.

- Tests are immutable to Claude and Codex (admin-only repo, CODEOWNERS-protected)
- Slice is **done** when golden tests pass — then **STOP**
- "Polish," "improve," "while we're at it" beyond passing = forbidden
- Test mutation attempts = HALT condition

### 5. Halt Conditions (mandatory, automatic)

Claude halts and surfaces to user when ANY of:

- Session pin SHA mismatch (any of 4 pinned files)
- Golden test mutation attempted by any agent
- Control-plane file edit attempted (`.github/`, `.codex/`, `polaris-controls/`, `scripts/autoloop/`)
- More than 2 cycles on the same problem
- Codex review unavailable for >1 hour
- PR exceeds 200 LOC
- Reviewer-fatigue indicators trip (>3 PRs queued for human in 24h per project)

### 6. Archive Discipline

Code in `POLARIS/.legacy/` is read-only reference.

- Never imported into production paths (CI gate enforces)
- Never modified post-cutover (CI gate enforces)
- New code lives in canonical paths (`src/`, `web/`, `tests/`, `docs/`, `.codex/`, `scripts/`)
- If old code is needed, it is RE-WRITTEN under the active slice, not copied from `.legacy/`

### 7. Visibility (no private channel exists)

All Claude↔Codex traffic is logged:

- `.codex/loop_log.jsonl` — streaming JSONL, tail-able real-time
- `.codex/slices/<slice_id>/brief.md` — Claude's question to Codex
- `.codex/slices/<slice_id>/verdict.md` — Codex's response
- `.codex/slices/<slice_id>/decision.md` — Claude's decision based on verdict
- `.codex/slices/<slice_id>/diff.patch` — resulting code (if any)

If an exchange is not in these files, it didn't legitimately happen.

### 8. Deviation Resolution

If any document conflicts with this Charter, this Charter wins.
If the Charter cannot be verified against its pinned SHA, Claude HALTS immediately.
Claude does not proceed on memory or interpretation.

---

## Session-Start Ritual (mechanical, every session, every restart)

```
1. Read POLARIS/docs/session_pin.txt
2. Run: gh api repos/aldrinor/polaris-controls/contents/CHARTER.md --jq .sha
   Compare returned blob SHA to pin file's charter_sha256
3. Same for PLAN.md, active slice spec, golden test manifest
4. ANY mismatch → emit halt notice to .codex/halt_<utc>.md; surface to user; do not proceed
5. ALL match → read CHARTER (this doc), PLAN, active slice, golden manifest
6. State to user: active slice ID + current step within slice + next action
7. Wait for user direction OR proceed if direction is unambiguous from slice spec
```

The ritual is enforced because the next PR fails Trusted App verification if any of these were skipped. The ritual is also enforced socially: the user reads the `.codex/` logs and sees whether the ritual ran.

---

## Plan Edit Path (the only legitimate way)

To change Charter, Plan, Slice spec, or Golden tests:

1. User edits the file in polaris-controls
2. User commits with GPG/SSH signature
3. User opens PR in POLARIS updating `docs/session_pin.txt` to the new SHA
4. Trusted App verifies new SHA matches polaris-controls HEAD
5. CI passes; user merges the pin update
6. Next session uses the new pinned versions

Claude never participates in plan edits. If Claude disagrees with a plan, Claude writes `.codex/proposal_<utc>.md` arguing the case and surfaces it. User decides.

---

## Out of Scope for Claude

Claude does NOT:

- Edit any file in `polaris-controls/`
- Edit `docs/session_pin.txt`
- Edit `.github/workflows/`, `.github/CODEOWNERS`
- Edit `state/authorized_signers.json` (when it exists)
- Sign any commit
- Merge any PR
- Mutate any test
- Touch `.legacy/` for any reason

If Claude attempts any of the above, the operation is rejected by hooks, branch protection, or both. There is no "but this is a special case."
